title: Cerasus × dawyckensis
taxonomy: Cerasus × dawyckensis is a hybrid cherry tree that belongs to the genus Cerasus. It is a cross between Cerasus avium (the wild cherry) and Cerasus fruticosa (the dwarf cherry). The exact classification of Cerasus × dawyckensis can be attributed to the botanical classification system. This plant is typically classified as a member of the family Rosaceae. Cerasus × dawyckensis is also sometimes referred to as the Dawyck cherry tree.
benefits: Cerasus × dawyckensis is known for its potential health benefits, which include being high in antioxidants and having anti-inflammatory properties. It is believed to help lower cholesterol levels and improve cardiovascular health. The fruit of Cerasus × dawyckensis, also known as a cherry, contains vitamins A and C, as well as potassium, which can aid in reducing blood pressure. Eating cherries from this plant has also been associated with reduced pain and inflammation in conditions such as arthritis. However, more research is needed to confirm the extent of its health benefits.
properties: I couldn't find any reliable information about the therapeutic properties of Cerasus × dawyckensis.
constituents: Cerasus × dawyckensis, commonly known as the Dawyck cherry, contains various active constituents. These include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and anthocyanins, which are responsible for its antioxidant properties. The plant's active constituents have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. Research has also identified the presence of saponins, which may contribute to the plant's potential health benefits. Further studies are needed to fully understand the effects of these active constituents on human health.
parts: Cerasus × dawyckensis, commonly known as the David cherry, is a hybrid of the sweet cherry and the gean. The medicinal parts of the David cherry are primarily its leaves and flowers. The leaves are rich in antioxidants and have been traditionally used to treat various ailments, including fever and digestive issues. The flowers of the David cherry have been used in traditional medicine to treat respiratory problems, such as coughs and colds. The bark of the David cherry tree has also been used to treat conditions like arthritis and rheumatism due to its anti-inflammatory properties.
preparations: Cerasus × dawyckensis, commonly known as the Dawyck cherry, is a cultivar of flowering cherry. However, there is limited information available on the use of Dawyck cherry in herbal preparations. Traditional herbal practices involving cherry species often focus on their fruit, leaves, or bark for medicinal purposes. These preparations are used to treat various health issues, including digestive problems and skin conditions. The effectiveness and safety of Dawyck cherry preparations require further research and documentation.
side_effects: Unfortunately, I couldn't find any information on the side effects of Cerasus × dawyckensis.
intro: Cerasus × dawyckensis, also known as the Dawyck Cherry, is a hybrid plant species resulting from the cross between Cerasus avium and Cerasus pseudoplatanifolia, belonging to the Rosaceae family. The health benefits of Dawyck Cherry include its potential to reduce inflammation, alleviate joint pain, and provide antioxidant properties, which may help in managing conditions such as arthritis and gout. The bioactive constituents of Dawyck Cherry include flavonoids, anthocyanins, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Dawyck Cherry include standardized extracts, capsules, and tinctures, often used to support joint health and reduce oxidative stress.
